# Extracao: transcription_mp4_02_2__Partner_Demonstration

In our workshops, people find it really helpful to see the partner role in action before they begin the process. Our goal here is simply to demonstrate how the conversation might flow. Peter will play the role of the partner, and Kim will be the storyteller. As Kim tells her story, watch for these four things. First, notice how Peter starts the conversation in a way that allows Kim to talk about what's important to her. Second, take note of the way Peter focuses on the emotions and feelings that came from the stories after getting some logistical details. Third, notice how Peter asks clarifying questions to invite Kim to keep sharing. And finally, pay attention to how Peter uses silence to allow Kim to process her thoughts and put them into words. Which one of those is forefront of your mind? Which springs to mind that you'd like to share with me now? I had a story that I had kind of forgotten about from when I was a kid, my brother being born. And your brother's name? Cody. Cody, right. When was Cody born then? He was born when I was six. Right, okay, so what sort of year was that? 1988. So what was it about that event that was so important for you? Well, it took forever for him to arrive. I mean, he was like in there for so long and every day I was waking up like, when's he coming out? So I guess like in kid world that was like a very long, exciting event that was building up. Okay. Thanks, Kim. Yeah. Although there may be more to explore from Kim's story, we'll stop there since this is just a demo. Let's look back at the four points I mentioned before. First, Peter opened the conversation by asking Kim to share a story that was in the forefront of her mind. This left the door open for Kim to take the conversation where she needed to go, not where Peter wanted her to go. Second, after Kim shared some logistical details, Peter spent the rest of the time asking Kim how she felt about her brother and the show and tell experience. Third, Peter did something really important. Rather than assume that he knew exactly what Kim meant by show and tell, he asked her a clarifying question to make sure he understood exactly what she meant. And finally, when Kim was searching for words to describe how she felt about her brother and the responsibility she felt for him, she was struggling. Rather than completing the sentence for her or asking another question, Peter waited quietly while Kim processed her thoughts so she could say how she really felt. Kim's other stories also share a common theme of nurturing and helping others to shine. And this theme is the core of her why statement, to support and encourage others so that they can revel in who they are. Now, if you had been Kim's partner, you may have asked different questions than Peter or taken the conversation in a different direction. And that's okay. There's no such thing as a right question. All roads lead to why. The important thing is to follow your gut and be curious. Now it's your turn. Ask your storyteller to share at least three specific stories with you as you listen, ask questions, and take notes.
